9:
"And Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian, which she had born unto Abraham, mocking." The witnesses note Ishmael's mocking — the crux of the offense. On "Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian... mocking," interpreters expound the act. The puritan Henry and the dominant reading (with Galatians 4:29) hold that Ishmael's "mocking" was a mocking and persecuting of Isaac: Ishmael (then about sixteen or seventeen) mocked, scoffed at, or derided Isaac (the heir of promise) — perhaps in envy and scorn at the feast (v.8), or persecuting him; Paul interprets it as persecution — "he that was born after the flesh persecuted him that was born after the Spirit" (Galatians 4:29); the mocking was a real offense, a scorning of the child of promise (and, some hold, mixed with profanity or idolatry); the carnal scoffing at the spiritual; a great sin. A milder reading takes the "mocking" (the Hebrew metsacheq, a play on Isaac's name, "laughing/playing") as mere playing or laughing — Ishmael playing (perhaps boisterously, or in a way that displeased Sarah), without necessarily malicious intent; but the dominant view, with Galatians 4:29 and the gravity of Sarah's response (v.10), takes it as mocking and persecuting.

On "the son of Hagar the Egyptian," the grammatical-historical note the contrast. Keil and Gill note that Sarah saw "the son of Hagar the Egyptian" (not "Ishmael," but pointedly the son of the bondwoman, the Egyptian) — the contrast between the son of the bondwoman (after the flesh) and Isaac the son of the free (after the promise); the offense that prompted Sarah's demand (v.10); the deliberate naming marking the distinction Paul would draw (Galatians 4:22-31).

On the mocking, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the consequence: Ishmael's mocking (whatever its exact form) was the occasion of his casting out (v.10); the scorn of the carnal seed against the spiritual; the persecution of the child of promise (Galatians 4:29); the act that, by God's overruling, led to the separation of the bondwoman and her son from the heir. The verse displays Ishmael's mocking.

13:
"And also of the son of the bondwoman will I make a nation, because he is thy seed." The brief verse notes God's promise to make Ishmael also a nation. The grammatical voices note the comfort. Ellicott and Gill note that "also of the son of the bondwoman will I make a nation, because he is thy seed" — God comforted Abraham (grieved for Ishmael, v.11) with the promise that Ishmael also (the son of the bondwoman) would be made a nation; the blessing on Ishmael (16:10; 17:20, twelve princes, a great nation); the assurance that, though not the heir of promise, Ishmael would not be unblessed.

On "will I make a nation," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the blessing. Calvin and Gill note that God would make Ishmael "a nation" — a great people (the Arab nations, 25:12-18); the temporal blessing on Ishmael for Abraham's sake; the kindness of God to the father's son, easing his grief at the parting.

On "because he is thy seed," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the ground: "because he is thy seed" — Ishmael, being Abraham's son (seed), would be blessed for Abraham's sake; the regard of God to the patriarch's offspring; though the covenant-line was through Isaac (v.12), Ishmael, as Abraham's seed, would become a nation; the comfort to the grieving father. The verse displays God's promise to make Ishmael a nation.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the comfort — "also of the son of the bondwoman will I make a nation," God comforting Abraham with the blessing on Ishmael (16:10; 17:20, Ellicott, Gill); "will I make a nation," the great nation of Ishmael (25:12-18, Calvin, Gill); "because he is thy seed," Ishmael blessed for Abraham's sake (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blessed Ishmael. The verse marks the promise: a nation. The verse displays the Ishmael: blessed. The verse displays the seed: Abraham's. The verse displays the comfort: to the father. The promise displays God blessing Ishmael for Abraham's sake. The verse displays the Ishmael: a nation. The verse displays the blessing: temporal. The verse displays the comfort: given. God's promise to make Ishmael also a nation displays the comfort to the grieving father: of the son of the bondwoman will I make a nation, because he is thy seed — Ishmael, though not the heir of promise, blessed for Abraham's sake with a great nation (17:20), the kindness that eased the father's grief. The verse displays the comforted Abraham, who sends Hagar away (v.14).
14:
"And Abraham rose up early in the morning, and took bread, and a bottle of water, and gave it unto Hagar, putting it on her shoulder, and the child, and sent her away: and she departed, and wandered in the wilderness of Beersheba." The witnesses note Abraham sending Hagar and Ishmael away. On "Abraham rose up early in the morning... and sent her away," the grammatical voices note the obedience. Ellicott and Gill note that "Abraham rose up early in the morning, and took bread, and a bottle of water, and gave it unto Hagar... and sent her away" — Abraham, in prompt obedience to God's word (v.12), rose early and sent Hagar and Ishmael away with provisions (bread and a bottle/skin of water); the patriarch's obedience, though grievous (v.11); the provision he made for them; the sending away of the bondwoman and her son.

On "putting it on her shoulder, and the child," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the departure. Henry and Gill note that Abraham put the provisions on Hagar's shoulder, and (sent away) the child; the humble departure of Hagar with Ishmael (now a youth); the reduced state of the bondwoman, sent forth with bread and water; the parting from Abraham's house.

On "she departed, and wandered in the wilderness of Beersheba," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the wandering: "she departed, and wandered in the wilderness of Beersheba" — Hagar went out and wandered (lost her way, or roamed) in the wilderness of Beersheba (the southern desert); the provisions soon spent (v.15); the distress of the wanderer; the setting for the deliverance by the angel (vv.17-19); the wilderness wandering of Hagar and Ishmael. The verse displays Abraham sending Hagar and Ishmael away.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the obedience — "Abraham rose up early... and sent her away," Abraham's prompt obedience, with provisions (v.12, Ellicott, Gill); "putting it on her shoulder, and the child," the humble departure (Henry, Gill); "she departed, and wandered in the wilderness of Beersheba," Hagar's wandering, the setting for the deliverance (vv.17-19, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sent Hagar. The verse marks the rising: early. The verse displays the provisions: bread and water. The verse displays the sending: away. The verse displays the wandering: in the wilderness. The obedience displays Abraham sending Hagar away with provision. The verse displays the Abraham: obeying. The verse displays the Hagar: departing. The verse displays the wilderness: wandered. Abraham sending Hagar and Ishmael away displays his prompt, grieved obedience: Abraham rose early, gave Hagar bread and water, and sent her away, and she wandered in the wilderness of Beersheba — the patriarch obeying God's word though grievous, the bondwoman and her son sent forth into the desert. The verse displays the wandering Hagar, her water spent (v.15).
15:
"And the water was spent in the bottle, and she cast the child under one of the shrubs." The brief verse notes the water spent and Hagar laying the child under a shrub. The grammatical voices note the distress. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the water was spent in the bottle, and she cast the child under one of the shrubs" — the water (their only drink) ran out in the wilderness, and Hagar, in desperation, laid (cast, placed) Ishmael under a shrub (a desert bush, for what little shade it gave); the extremity of their distress — water gone, the youth fainting (from thirst, perhaps ill); the mother's anguish.

On "the water was spent," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the extremity. Clarke and Ellicott note that the spending of the water brought them to the brink of death; the desperate condition in the desert; the trial of Hagar and Ishmael at their lowest; the setting for God's deliverance (vv.17-19).

On "she cast the child under one of the shrubs," the grammatical-historical note the despair: "she cast the child under one of the shrubs" — Hagar laid Ishmael (now fainting) under a bush, unable to carry him further or bear to watch him die; the mother's despairing act; the youth left in the shade of a shrub; the picture of human extremity, on the edge of death, where God's help would come. The verse displays the water spent and Hagar laying the child under a shrub.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the distress — "the water was spent in the bottle, and she cast the child under one of the shrubs," the water gone, Hagar laying Ishmael under a bush (Keil, Gill); the extremity of their condition (Clarke, Ellicott); "she cast the child under one of the shrubs," the despairing act, the setting for deliverance (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the distressed Hagar. The verse marks the water: spent. The verse displays the child: under a shrub. The verse displays the distress: extreme. The verse displays the despair: of the mother. The distress displays Hagar and Ishmael at the brink of death. The verse displays the water: gone. The verse displays the Ishmael: laid down. The verse displays the extremity: reached. The water spent and Hagar laying the child under a shrub display the extremity of distress: the water was spent, and she cast the child under a shrub — the desperate condition in the desert, the fainting youth laid in the shade, on the edge of death where God's help would come (vv.17-19). The verse displays the despairing Hagar, weeping (v.16).
16:
"And she went, and sat her down over against him a good way off, as it were a bowshot: for she said, Let me not see the death of the child. And she sat over against him, and lift up her voice, and wept." The witnesses note Hagar's despair as she weeps for the dying child. On "she went, and sat her down over against him a good way off," the grammatical voices note the despair. Ellicott and Gill note that "she went, and sat her down over against him a good way off, as it were a bowshot: for she said, Let me not see the death of the child" — Hagar withdrew a little distance (a bowshot) and sat down opposite Ishmael, unable to bear watching him die; the mother's anguish, removing herself from the sight of her child's death; the depth of her despair.

On "Let me not see the death of the child," the reformed and witnesses note the anguish. Gill notes that Hagar said, "Let me not see the death of the child" — her dread of watching Ishmael die; the helpless grief of the mother; the abandonment of hope; the extremity of the mother's sorrow.

On "she... lift up her voice, and wept," the grammatical-historical note the weeping: "she sat over against him, and lift up her voice, and wept" — Hagar wept aloud in her despair; the mother's tears for her dying son; the grief that God would hear and answer (v.17, "God heard the voice of the lad"); the weeping that preceded the deliverance. The verse displays Hagar's despair as she weeps for the dying child.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the despair — "she sat her down over against him a good way off... Let me not see the death of the child," Hagar withdrawing, unable to watch Ishmael die (Ellicott, Gill); "Let me not see the death of the child," the mother's anguish (Gill); "she lift up her voice, and wept," the weeping before the deliverance (v.17, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the weeping Hagar. The verse marks the distance: a bowshot. The verse displays the dread: of the death. The verse displays the weeping: aloud. The verse displays the despair: of the mother. The despair displays Hagar's anguish for her dying son. The verse displays the Hagar: weeping. The verse displays the child: dying. The verse displays the grief: deep. Hagar's despair as she weeps for the dying child displays the extremity of her sorrow: she sat a bowshot away, saying, Let me not see the death of the child, and wept — the mother's anguish, unable to watch Ishmael die, weeping aloud, the grief that God would hear and answer (v.17). The verse displays the weeping Hagar, heard by God (v.17).
17:
"And God heard the voice of the lad; and the angel of God called to Hagar out of heaven, and said unto her, What aileth thee, Hagar? fear not; for God hath heard the voice of the lad where he is." The witnesses note God hearing the lad and the angel comforting Hagar. On "God heard the voice of the lad," the grammatical voices note the hearing. Ellicott and Gill note that "God heard the voice of the lad" — God heard the cry of Ishmael (the lad, perhaps crying out in his distress, or his cry going up to God); fittingly, "Ishmael" means "God hears" (16:11); the God who hears the afflicted heard the dying youth; the deliverance prompted by God's hearing.

On "the angel of God called to Hagar out of heaven... What aileth thee, Hagar? fear not," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the comfort. Wesley and Gill note that "the angel of God called to Hagar out of heaven... What aileth thee, Hagar? fear not" — the angel called from heaven, asking Hagar what troubled her, and bidding her not fear; the comfort of God to the despairing mother (as the Angel had found her before, 16:7-13); the gracious word "fear not" to the weeping Hagar.

On "God hath heard the voice of the lad where he is," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the assurance: "God hath heard the voice of the lad where he is" — God had heard Ishmael where he lay (under the shrub, v.15); the assurance that God's eye and ear were on the dying youth; the deliverance at hand; the comfort that God had not forsaken them; the regard of God to the afflicted lad. The verse displays God hearing the lad and the angel comforting Hagar.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the hearing — "God heard the voice of the lad," God hearing Ishmael's cry (16:11, Ellicott, Gill); "What aileth thee, Hagar? fear not," the angel's comfort (16:7-13, Wesley, Gill); "God hath heard the voice of the lad where he is," the assurance of God's regard (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the hearing God. The verse marks the hearing: of the lad. The verse displays the angel: calling. The verse displays the comfort: fear not. The verse displays the comforted Hagar, bidden to lift up the lad (v.18).
18:
"Arise, lift up the lad, and hold him in thine hand; for I will make him a great nation." The brief verse notes the angel bidding Hagar lift the lad, with the promise of a great nation. The grammatical voices note the command. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Arise, lift up the lad, and hold him in thine hand; for I will make him a great nation" — the angel bade Hagar arise, lift up Ishmael (raise and support him), and hold him; the comfort and the action commanded; the assurance, with the renewed promise that Ishmael would become a great nation (16:10; 17:20; 21:13).

On "lift up the lad, and hold him in thine hand," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the encouragement. Calvin and Gill note that Hagar was to lift up and hold Ishmael — to take heart and care for him; the encouragement to the despairing mother; the action that, with God's provision (the well, v.19), would save the youth; the rousing of hope.

On "I will make him a great nation," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the promise: "I will make him a great nation" — the renewed promise that Ishmael would become a great nation (the Arab peoples, 25:12-18); the assurance that the dying youth would live and prosper; the ground of the comfort; God's purpose for Ishmael (for Abraham's sake, v.13). The verse displays the angel bidding Hagar lift the lad, with the promise.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the command — "Arise, lift up the lad, and hold him in thine hand," the angel bidding Hagar raise and hold Ishmael (Keil, Gill); the encouragement to the mother (Calvin, Gill); "I will make him a great nation," the renewed promise (16:10; 17:20, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the encouraged Hagar. The verse marks the command: lift up the lad. The verse displays the holding: in her hand. The verse displays the promise: a great nation. The verse displays the encouraged Hagar, shown the well (v.19).
19:
"And God opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water; and she went, and filled the bottle with water, and gave the lad drink." The witnesses note God opening Hagar's eyes to see the well. On "God opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water," the grammatical voices note the provision. Ellicott and Gill note that "God opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water" — God enabled Hagar to see a well of water (whether one already there, which she had not seen in her distress, now revealed to her; or, by some, a well newly provided); the deliverance — water in the desert; God's provision for the dying; the opening of her eyes to the help at hand.

On "God opened her eyes," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the seeing. Clarke and Gill note that "God opened her eyes" — God directed Hagar's sight to the well (which her grief and despair had kept her from seeing, or which God now made visible); the provision was God's gift; the deliverance from death; the eyes opened to the water of life (a figure, by some, of God opening the eyes to see his provision and grace).

On "she went, and filled the bottle with water, and gave the lad drink," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the deliverance: "she filled the bottle with water, and gave the lad drink" — Hagar filled the skin and gave Ishmael drink; the youth revived; the deliverance from death by God's provision; the saving of Ishmael; the mercy of God to the afflicted. The verse displays God opening Hagar's eyes to see the well.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the provision — "God opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water," God revealing the well (Ellicott, Gill); "God opened her eyes," directing her sight to the help (Clarke, Gill); "she filled the bottle... and gave the lad drink," the deliverance of Ishmael (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the provided well. The verse marks the eyes: opened. The verse displays the well: seen. The verse displays the water: drawn. The verse displays the lad: revived. 20:
"And God was with the lad; and he grew, and dwelt in the wilderness, and became an archer." The witnesses note God's being with Ishmael as he grew in the wilderness. On "God was with the lad," the grammatical voices note the providence. Ellicott and Gill note that "God was with the lad" — God's presence and blessing were with Ishmael; the providence and care of God over the youth (for Abraham's sake, v.13; and by the promise, 16:10; 17:20); Ishmael, though not the heir of promise, watched over and blessed by God; the temporal favor of God on the lad.

On "he grew, and dwelt in the wilderness," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the growth. Calvin and Gill note that Ishmael "grew, and dwelt in the wilderness" — he grew up and dwelt in the desert (the wild, free life of the Bedouin, fulfilling the word that he would be a wild man, 16:12); the prophecy of his character fulfilled; the youth thriving in the wilderness under God's care.

On "became an archer," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the life: "became an archer" — Ishmael became an archer (a hunter, a man of the bow); the free, roving, hunting life of the desert; the character of the wild man (16:12) and his descendants (the Arabs); the youth, delivered and blessed, growing into the man of the wilderness. The verse displays God's being with Ishmael as he grew.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the providence — "God was with the lad," God's care over Ishmael (v.13; 17:20, Ellicott, Gill); "he grew, and dwelt in the wilderness," the wild, free life fulfilling 16:12 (Calvin, Gill); "became an archer," the hunting life of the desert (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the thriving Ishmael. The verse marks the God: with him. The verse displays the wilderness: dwelt in. The verse displays the archer: became. The verse displays the grown Ishmael, in Paran (v.21).
21:
"And he dwelt in the wilderness of Paran: and his mother took him a wife out of the land of Egypt." The brief verse notes Ishmael dwelling in Paran and his Egyptian wife. The grammatical voices note the dwelling. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "he dwelt in the wilderness of Paran: and his mother took him a wife out of the land of Egypt" — Ishmael settled in the wilderness of Paran (a desert region south of Canaan, toward Sinai/Arabia); his mother Hagar (herself an Egyptian, 16:1) took him a wife from Egypt; the establishing of Ishmael in the desert, with an Egyptian wife; the founding of the Ishmaelite/Arab line.

On "the wilderness of Paran," the witnesses note the region. Gill notes that Paran was a desert region (where Israel later wandered, Numbers 10:12); Ishmael's dwelling-place; the wild, desert home of the Ishmaelites; the region of the Arab peoples.

On "his mother took him a wife out of the land of Egypt," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the marriage: "his mother took him a wife out of the land of Egypt" — Hagar, an Egyptian, chose an Egyptian wife for Ishmael; the Ishmaelite line, through this marriage, mixed with Egyptian blood; the founding of Ishmael's house; the separation from the line of promise (the wife not of Abraham's kindred, as Isaac's would be, ch. 24); Ishmael established apart from the covenant-line. The verse displays Ishmael dwelling in Paran and his Egyptian wife.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the dwelling — "he dwelt in the wilderness of Paran," Ishmael settling in the desert (Keil, Gill); the wild, desert home of the Ishmaelites (Numbers 10:12, Gill); "his mother took him a wife out of the land of Egypt," the Egyptian marriage, Ishmael apart from the covenant-line (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the settled Ishmael. The verse marks the wilderness: Paran. The verse displays the wife: Egyptian. The verse displays the line: Ishmael's. The verse closes the history of Hagar and Ishmael, before the covenant with Abimelech (vv.22-34).
22:
"And it came to pass at that time, that Abimelech and Phichol the chief captain of his host spake unto Abraham, saying, God is with thee in all that thou doest:" The witnesses note Abimelech's acknowledgment that God was with Abraham. On "Abimelech and Phichol the chief captain of his host spake unto Abraham," the grammatical voices note the approach. Ellicott and Gill note that "Abimelech and Phichol the chief captain of his host spake unto Abraham" — Abimelech (the king of Gerar, ch. 20) and Phichol (his army-commander) came to Abraham to make a covenant (vv.23-32); the king seeking a treaty with the prospering patriarch; the approach of the Philistine ruler.

On "God is with thee in all that thou doest," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the acknowledgment. Henry and Calvin note that Abimelech said, "God is with thee in all that thou doest" — the heathen king acknowledged that God was with Abraham, prospering him in all things; the testimony of the pagan to God's blessing on the patriarch (as God had said, God was with him); the recognition of Abraham's favor with God; the witness of the world to the blessing on the godly.

On the acknowledgment, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the reason for the covenant: Abimelech, perceiving God's blessing on Abraham (and perhaps remembering the affair of ch. 20), sought a covenant of peace and good faith with the prospering, God-favored patriarch; the king's prudence in securing the friendship of one so blessed; the providence that gave Abraham favor and influence among the Philistines. The verse displays Abimelech's acknowledgment that God was with Abraham.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the approach — "Abimelech and Phichol... spake unto Abraham," the king and his captain coming to make a covenant (vv.23-32, Ellicott, Gill); "God is with thee in all that thou doest," the heathen king's acknowledgment of God's blessing on Abraham (Henry, Calvin); the king seeking covenant with the God-favored patriarch (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the acknowledging Abimelech. The verse marks the approach: Abimelech and Phichol. The verse displays the acknowledgment: God is with thee. The verse displays the acknowledging Abimelech, seeking an oath (v.23).
23:
"Now therefore swear unto me here by God that thou wilt not deal falsely with me, nor with my son, nor with my son's son: but according to the kindness that I have done unto thee, thou shalt do unto me, and to the land wherein thou hast sojourned." The witnesses note Abimelech seeking an oath of good faith from Abraham. On "swear unto me here by God that thou wilt not deal falsely with me," the grammatical voices note the oath. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "swear unto me here by God that thou wilt not deal falsely with me, nor with my son, nor with my son's son" — Abimelech asked Abraham to swear (by God) that he would deal honestly with him and his descendants; the request for a covenant of good faith, binding for the future; the king securing peace between his line and Abraham's.

On "nor with my son, nor with my son's son," the witnesses note the perpetuity. Gill notes that the oath was to extend to Abimelech's posterity ("my son, my son's son") — a lasting covenant of good faith between the two houses; the king's care for his descendants; the perpetual treaty.

On "according to the kindness that I have done unto thee, thou shalt do unto me," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the appeal: "according to the kindness that I have done unto thee" — Abimelech appealed to the kindness he had shown Abraham (the welcome and gifts, 20:14-15; the freedom to dwell in his land); he asked the like kindness in return; the basis of the covenant in mutual good faith; the just request of the king. The verse displays Abimelech seeking an oath of good faith.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the oath — "swear unto me here by God that thou wilt not deal falsely with me, nor with my son, nor with my son's son," Abimelech asking an oath of good faith for the future (Keil, Gill); the perpetual covenant extending to his posterity (Gill); "according to the kindness that I have done unto thee," the appeal to mutual kindness (20:14-15, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the covenant-seeking Abimelech. The verse marks the oath: swear by God. The verse displays the good faith: requested. The verse displays the posterity: included. 24:
"And Abraham said, I will swear." The very brief verse notes Abraham's consent to the oath. The grammatical voices note the agreement. Gill and Calvin note that "Abraham said, I will swear" — Abraham consented to Abimelech's request, agreeing to take the oath of good faith; the patriarch's readiness to make the covenant of peace; the agreement to deal honestly with Abimelech and his house (v.23); the consent to the treaty.

On the oath, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the peace: Abraham's consent to the oath established the covenant of peace and good faith with Abimelech; the patriarch securing peace with the Philistine king; the lawful oath ratifying the treaty; the godly man dealing honestly and peaceably with his neighbor (Romans 12:18); the consent that led to the covenant (vv.27-32). The verse displays Abraham's consent to the oath.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the consent — "Abraham said, I will swear," Abraham agreeing to the oath (Gill, Calvin); the lawful use of an oath (v.23, Gill); the consent establishing the covenant of peace (Romans 12:18, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the consenting Abraham. The verse marks the consent: I will swear. The verse displays the oath: agreed. The verse displays the peace: secured. The verse displays the covenant: begun. The consent displays Abraham agreeing to the covenant of good faith. The verse displays the Abraham: consenting. The verse displays the oath: taken. The verse displays the peace: made. The verse displays the consenting Abraham, who first reproves about the well (v.25).
25:
"And Abraham reproved Abimelech because of a well of water, which Abimelech's servants had violently taken away." The brief verse notes Abraham reproving Abimelech over the seized well.
